Understanding Your Role

Welcome to the Georgia Urology Patient Support Center!

Your role as a Patient Support Center Representative is a multi-leveled
problem-solving position. Once you are released to the “floor” to start taking
calls on your own, you will be expected to create positive and patient focused
experiences. You are the first point-of-contact for our patients, which means
what you do has a huge impact! Think of yourself as a patient liaison,
connecting callers to needed services and providing guidance through the
appointment making process.

Patient Support Center Representatives are Expected to:

Demonstrate Positive Customer Service + Use Resources Critically and
Creatively - Effectively Communicate with Patients and Staff « Take
Ownership

Customer Service
2.1 Active Listening

One of the most important skills you can have in the medical scheduling setting is
listening. From accurately creating patient's chart to messaging clinical staff about life
changing medication, everything falls back to being an active and resourceful listener
for your caller. Active listening promotes positive engagement and ensures that you
fully tune in to your caller's wants and can anticipate their needs. This is a skill that

requires intentional practice in your customer service routine.

Active listening is the conscious practice of non-judgmental
listening with goal of clarity and understanding.

2.2 Confidence and Foresight

Having confidence is an especially important trait develop as you problem solve our
callers’ requests. Knowledge or confidence in your services and product make an
immense difference in trust that your customers have in your company. Being
prepared to answer questions before they are even asked can really enhance that level of
trust.

Example A: A patient states that they live in a city that you have never heard of
before. As soon as you hear that city's name, look it up on Google maps and
determine which offices are closest and most convenient. Doing so creates
better a chance the patient will be impressed you alrea

e. As soon as you hear that city's name, look it up on Google maps and
determine which offices are closest and most convenient. Doing so creates
better a chance the patient will be impressed you already know which location
would be ideal.
Example B: A patient would like to be seen at a specific office that is rarely
visited by the desired provider. There will not be an opening for quite some time.
After finding the first availability at that location, also find a sooner availability at
another nearby office. Having this additional option ready will indirectly inform
the caller that you have gone the extra mile by having a “Plan B” at their
disposal.
Some ways you increase your confidence in this role are

Familiarizing yourself with trends in the physicians’ schedule

- Having a clear grasp on using the Preference Guideline and Insurance Grid +

Identifying additional resources that will support you while scheduling

(Floor support, the Georgia Urology website, emails announcing updated
information, etc.)

+ Organizing notes and creating a record of answers to questions you come across

As your confidence grows in this position your foresight, the ability to
plan and anticipate customer needs, will grow also!

2.3 Self- Awareness

Self-awareness is your ability to identify your actions and motivations. This skill
especially crucial as you navigate solving patient inquiries and issues. Throughout of
your shift you are expected to treat every call like it is your first

call of

ctions and motivations. This skill
especially crucial as you navigate solving patient inquiries and issues. Throughout of
your shift you are expected to treat every call like it is your first

call of the day. This means maintaining a consistent level of welcoming enthusiasm and
openness with your callers. Having an increased self-awareness can support your ability
to perform successfully and create positive experiences during the day.

You can break self-awareness down into three levels:
Thinking- identifying thoughts and emotions in order to understand and adjust
mental reactions o Can be achieved with meditation, self-questioning, and
emotion labeling for example
Voicing- organizing your thoughts, beliefs, and attitudes with a visual medium
o Can be achieved with writing, completing personality tests, and vision boards
for example
Implementing- analyzing past actions and deciding how to handle future

situations o Can be achieved with honest self-reflections, feedback from trusted

people, and performance evaluations for example

Check in with yourself throughout the day! You can self-assess your current
'thoughts/emotions, beliefs/attitudes, and behaviors/decisions. Taking inventory of
your mental state can help you prevent errors, avoid burnout, master your

resources, and remain solution oriented.

Greetings and Wrap Ups
3.1 Incoming Calls

There are three main components to the standard incoming call greeting, each serving a
specific purpose. Remember you are the first point of contact for the caller and this
may be their first experience with Georgia Urology. We must intentionally
communicate our focus on patient care from the start.

On an incoming call your introduction would include three primary components.
Thank you for calling Georgia Urology.

o We must brand the call. This also confirms with the caller that they have

reached the right place for assistance.
This is_(yourname) _.

o We must identify ourselves on every call, there are no exceptions. Patients
appreciate the opportunity to speak with a live person when making an
appointment or seeking a solution to an inquiry. Sharing our names create
a personable experience for the caller.

* Whom dol have the pleasure of speaking with?

o Please make note of this information and use caller's name throughout the
call. Having the caller identify themselves serves multiple purposes. Using
the caller's name creates a positive connection during your interaction.
Additionally, names serve as the fo

practice. Sometimes a caller is scheduling an appointment or requesting

information on a patient's behalf, and we need that caller's identification

to verify HIPPA authorization. Once you confirm the caller's name and it to

3.2 Calls Backs
Callers trying to reach our services have the option to request a “call back” from
us. Instead of waiting on hold until they can speak with someone, our system will save
their place in the queue line and make an outgoing call to their number.

As you are answering calls, you will be able to recognize a call back when you hear an

automated voice say the phrase, “Please wait while the call is established.” In these
occurrences, we want to verbally acknowledge that our system is making an outgoing
call to the patient by adopting to the following greeting:

“This is (your name) from Georgia Urology returning your call. How

can I help you?”

Sometimes the caller may not be available at the time of the call. In that case please
leave the following message on the caller's voicemail:

“This is Georgia Urology returning your call if you still need assistance please

call us back, we are experiencing longer

. In that case please
leave the following message on the caller's voicemail:

“This is Georgia Urology returning your call if you still need assistance please

call us back, we are experiencing longer than normal wait times but we ask that
you hold for an agent. We appreciate your patience and thank you for choosing
Georgia Urology.”


3.3 Outbound Calls

There are times when you will need to make an outbound call to get in touch with a
patient to reschedule, confirm information, or reconnect with a lost call. In those cases,

you will still introduce yourself, state your connection with Georgia Urology, as well as
confirm the identity of the answering individual.

“Good morning/Good afternoon this is (your name) with Georgia
Urology. Am I speaking with Mr./Ms. 2”

For Pediatric calls:

“Good morning/Good afternoon this is (your name) with Georgia Urology. Is
this a parent or legal guardian of ? May I ask your name for my

| records please?”

3.4 Dropped Calls

When we experience technical issues that cause the phone system to drop calls, we
need to have a way to alert callers. This is not a full-proof method, but we hope that if
the caller is attempting to call us right back, we will have the Opportunity to explain the
problem that occurred with a consistent and professional response.

After we have system crash that causes us to lose a call (that could be actively talking,
just starting the call, or the phone was starting to ring on your line). When we answer
the next activ

ponse.

After we have system crash that causes us to lose a call (that could be actively talking,
just starting the call, or the phone was starting to ring on your line). When we answer
the next active call, we need to change to the following greeting:

“Thank you for calling Georgia Urology. This is (your name) _. I am sorry we just

experienced a system issue that resulted in us losing calls. I apologize for any

inconvenience this may have cause you. How can I help you?”

In addition to this greeting, obtain the patient's name and a contact number in case the

technical issue persists, and you must reach back out the patient.


3.5 Dead Air

Due to growing use of various call technologies (computer-based phone lines, cell
phones, and blue tooth) we sometimes experience “dead air calls.” In order to ensure
that we give our patients every opportunity to connect with us we will use the
following dead air call protocol:

* Use the standard phone greeting --- Wait 30 seconds
* Do a second abbreviated greeting --- Georgia Urology this is (your name)
77> Wait another 30 seconds

* Simply say Hello --- Wait another 30 seconds

We now have given the caller a minute and a half to see if we have connectivity.
* End the call with the following statement:

+ Disconnecting call due to no response.

4.1 Transferring Calls

Sometimes patients have requests that fall outside the scope of the Patient Support

Center. In those cases, you will need to transfer that caller to the appropriate
department for further assistance.

Note that before you reach out to another department or Floor Support you
must create a chart for the patient in NextGen. This will aid the receiving party
| in best assisting the situation.

* Warm Transfers

Most transfers will be warm transfers, which means that you make contact with a
department representative on the caller’s behalf, explain the caller’s needs, and
| then release the call. To ensure that a patient has the best service experience it is
critical for you to make contact with the right department and prepare both the caller
and the receiver for a call transfer.

department representative. If the department you are trying to reach is
currently unavailable, you will hear an explanatory message. Unfortunately, we

do not have the capability to wait on hold for extended periods u

* Vasectomy Consultations

One of the most common types of appointment you will schedule are vasectomy
consultation. Often patients are not aware of Georgia Urology’s process in scheduling
this procedure. For patients interested in vasectomies they must first schedule a
consultation to discuss the procedure with a physician. Procedure appointments will
then be handled with the Ambulatory Surgery Center of that physician's pod.

Please state the following when scheduling a vasectomy consultation:

I would like to clarify that this appointment is a consultation with the physician.
This visit is for you to discuss the details of the vasectomy procedure and receive
clarification to any questions you may have. If you decide to move forward with the
procedure after your consultation, the physician will then discuss the scheduling
process. The procedure

can usually be scheduled for 6-8 weeks later.

before

* Circumcision Consultations
The pediatric side of the practice receive numerous requests for circumcisions. Like
vasectomies, any patient needing a circumcision must have a consultation first
before a procedure can be scheduled. It is very important that parents/legal
guardians/physician's office understand that when they arrive to that initial
appointment, they need to expect a consultation nota procedure.

Please state the following when scheduling a circumcision consultation:

I would like to clarify that this appointment is a consultation with the
physician.

This visit is to determine if your child is a surgical candidate for a circumcision.
Surgery will not be performed on this day. If your child is determined to be a surgical
candidate, the physician will then discuss the process of scheduling the procedure.

The procedure can usually be scheduled for 6-8 weeks later.

office, their responsibilities are typically deferred to either another surgery nurse,

Schedulers.

9.2 Insurance Types

If a caller is having a difficult time identifying if their insurance type, direct them to look
at the bottom corners of their card. If the caller still struggles default to PPO, the Central
Business Office will verify patient insurance information once it is in our system.

* HMO (Health Maintenance Organization)- Have one in-network PCP that
provides routine care and refers you to in-network specialists. Referrals are required,
and out-of-network care will not be covered.

EPO (Exclusive Provider Organization) - Similar to HMO, only in-network care is
covered. Generally, have larger networks than HMO. Similar to PPO, no referrals
required for specialist care.

POS (Point of Service)- Have one in-network PCP that provides routine care and
manages your access to other doctors. Referrals to specialists are required. You can
also visit other out-of-network physicians at a higher cost.

PPO (Preferred Provider Organization)- Not required to have a PCP and do not
need referrals to see specialists. You can choose which doctors you see. Out-

ofnetwork phy

physicians at a higher cost.

PPO (Preferred Provider Organization)- Not required to have a PCP and do not
need referrals to see specialists. You can choose which doctors you see. Out-

ofnetwork physicians will cost more.

11.1 Gathering Key Information

As a urologic specialist group, we receive an abundant amount of kidney stone referrals
on a regular basis. Kidney stones vary in size, shape and pain levels. Because there are
so many variables involved with kidney stones, it is crucial that we ask patients or
referring providers offices the proper questions.

Have you had a kidney stone before?

What is your current comfort level?

Has the patient had any recent imaging done? If so, where? Do you have a copy

of the CT report/disc of the imaging you've had done? If not, can you bring that

information/have the information faxed for your evaluation?

How large is the kidney stone?

Are you experiencing any other symptoms?

o If the patient describes any nausea or visible blood in urine, create chart

first then transfer this call to triage

Getting these questions answered makes treating the patient significantly easier. If you
are unable to find an immediate appointment for the patient, especially if
experiencing severe symptoms, reach out to Floor Support or Triage. Make sure the
patient has a chart in our system before you reach out to another department!

Chapter 1: Your Role

As a pediatric patient support specialist, you are expected to create positive
and patient-focused experiences. Interactions will be with parents, legal
guardians, and pediatric offices to facilitate the care of a minor child. At
times, the parents may not know the reason for the appointment. It is our
responsibility to ask the correct probing questions to determine what the visit
reason or diagnosis is and with whom to schedule the appointment with.

Chapter 2: Greetings and Closing

2.1 Incoming Calls

We use the standard Patient Support Center Greeting but acknowledge that the caller has
reached a pediatric agent when we see PEDS 1111 on our Mitel phone. The greeting is as
follows: “Thank you for calling Georgia Urology Pediatrics. You’ve reached the Patient
Support Center. This is (your name here). How can | assist you?”

2.2 Who can make a PEDS appointmen

2.2 Who can make a PEDS appointment?

Anyone over the age of 18 can call and schedule an appointment. Most callers will be
parents, legal guardians, and in some circumstances the pediatric office. Always note on
the appointment details the name and the relationship of the person you are speaking
with. When speaking with a doctor’s office, note the name of the person calling and the
name of the office. If the patient is over the age of 18 but is still seeing one of our pediatric
doctors, then the parent/legal guardian must call to schedule the appointment.

New Patients

guardian or foster parent is bringing the child, legal guardianship or foster paperworkis
required. Stepparents cannot bring a child to the initial visit alone; it mustbea legal

guardian, such as an adoptive parent.

Check with the front desk as certain appointments require the parent to be present.

5.3 Prenatal Consultations

This is when expecting mothers are referred to see a Urologist for abnormalities
found in the fetus. These abnormalities are typically diagnosed as Hydronephrosis,
which means swelling of the kidney(s) when too much urine collects in the pelvis (or
basin) of the kidney. Most often discovered with a routine maternal-fetal
ultrasound. Once discovered, the mother should be referred to a fetal medicine
specialist or pediatric urologist between 20 and 30 weeks of pregnancy.

When scheduling a prenatal consultation, you must create a chart using the mom's
information, not the unborn child’s. Use the Prenatal Hydronephrosis chief
complaint when scheduling in Dash. Schedule the appointment at least a week out
to allow time to gather records and disk. Always advise the expecting mother to
bring the disk to their appointment. If they are having a virtual appointment, the disk
must be mailed to the attention of the provider's MA.

After the baby is born, the parent will call back to schedule a “follow-up”
appointment. Create a new chart using the child’s information and schedule them
with the same provider the mom was evaluated by. Verify that postnatal scans have
been completed, then email the provider’s MA t

MD (DOCTOR) VS ADVANCED PRACTITIONER (PA or NP) -

e MD-a Medical Doctor or Doctor of Medicine; has a higher level of
degree than a PA (Physician Assistant) or NP (Nurse Practitioner)

e APP-Alicensed medical professional that provides medical care
and performs similar functions as the MD. An APP would commonly
be known as a PAor NP, see below:

e PA- Physician Assistant; a healthcare professional who works
under the supervision of a physician to provide patient care

e NP-Nurse Practitioner; A registered nurse with advanced
education and clinic training in a specific area of nursing

*Itis crucial that these terms are used accurately*
